overview.title: Raspberry Pi Resources
overview.description: Complete guide to Raspberry Pi single-board computers, HATs, sensors, and accessories for computing, IoT, and embedded projects

boards.name: Raspberry Pi 5
boards.shortDesc: Latest flagship Pi with 2.4GHz quad-core processor, PCIe 2.0, and up to 8GB RAM
boards.fullDescription: The Raspberry Pi 5 is the most powerful Pi yet, featuring a custom 2.4GHz quad-core processor, dual 4K display support, PCIe 2.0 interface for NVMe drives and AI accelerators, and improved I/O performance. Perfect for desktop computing, AI projects, and demanding applications.

codeSnippet.title: GPIO LED Blink
codeSnippet.code:
# Raspberry Pi GPIO LED blink example
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

LED_PIN = 17

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)

try:
    while True:
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)
        time.sleep(1)
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)
        time.sleep(1)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    GPIO.cleanup()

boards.name: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B
boards.shortDesc: Popular Pi with 1.5GHz quad-core CPU, dual 4K displays, and up to 8GB RAM
boards.fullDescription: The Raspberry Pi 4 Model B offers desktop-level performance with a quad-core processor, up to 8GB RAM, dual display support, and USB 3.0. It's the most popular choice for hobbyists, educators, and professionals building everything from home servers to IoT devices.

codeSnippet.title: Temperature Monitoring

boards.name: Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W
boards.shortDesc: Ultra-compact Pi with quad-core CPU, WiFi, and Bluetooth in tiny form factor
boards.fullDescription: The Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W packs a quad-core processor, built-in WiFi and Bluetooth into a compact 65mm x 30mm board. Perfect for embedded projects, wearables, and space-constrained applications where size matters.

specifications.USB: 1x micro USB OTG, 1x micro USB power

codeSnippet.title: WiFi Connection Status
codeSnippet.code:
# Check WiFi connection status
import subprocess

def check_wifi():
    try:
        result = subprocess.check_output(['iwgetid', '-r'])
        ssid = result.decode('utf-8').strip()
        print(f"Connected to: {ssid}")
        return True
    except:
        print("Not connected to WiFi")
        return False

check_wifi()

boards.name: Raspberry Pi Pico
boards.shortDesc: Low-cost microcontroller board with RP2040 chip, dual-core ARM Cortex-M0+ at 133MHz
boards.fullDescription: The Raspberry Pi Pico is a low-cost, high-performance microcontroller board featuring the RP2040 chip designed by Raspberry Pi. With dual-core ARM Cortex-M0+ processor, flexible I/O, and comprehensive SDK support, it's perfect for embedded projects, physical computing, and learning microcontroller programming.
useCases: Embedded systems and IoT edge devices

codeSnippet.title: MicroPython Blink Example
codeSnippet.code:
# Raspberry Pi Pico LED blink with MicroPython
from machine import Pin
import time

led = Pin(25, Pin.OUT)  # Built-in LED on GP25

while True:
    led.toggle()
    time.sleep(0.5)

boards.name: Raspberry Pi Pico W
boards.shortDesc: Pico with 2.4GHz WiFi and Bluetooth 5.2 for wireless IoT projects
boards.fullDescription: The Raspberry Pi Pico W adds wireless connectivity to the popular Pico board, featuring 2.4 GHz 802.11n WiFi and Bluetooth 5.2 via the Infineon CYW43439 chip. Perfect for IoT projects requiring internet connectivity while maintaining the same powerful RP2040 microcontroller and compact form factor.

codeSnippet.title: WiFi Connection Example
codeSnippet.code:
# Connect Pico W to WiFi with MicroPython
import network
import time

wlan = network.WLAN(network.STA_IF)
wlan.active(True)
wlan.connect('YOUR_SSID', 'YOUR_PASSWORD')

# Wait for connection
max_wait = 10
while max_wait > 0:
    if wlan.status() < 0 or wlan.status() >= 3:
        break
    max_wait -= 1
    print('Waiting for connection...')
    time.sleep(1)

if wlan.status() != 3:
    raise RuntimeError('Network connection failed')
else:
    print('Connected!')
    status = wlan.ifconfig()
    print('IP:', status[0])

boards.name: Raspberry Pi Pico 2
boards.shortDesc: Advanced Pico with RP2350, dual-core ARM Cortex-M33 or RISC-V at 150MHz, enhanced security
boards.fullDescription: The Raspberry Pi Pico 2 features the new RP2350 microcontroller with selectable dual-core ARM Cortex-M33 or RISC-V Hazard3 processors running at 150MHz. It offers doubled memory, comprehensive security features including Arm TrustZone, and maintains full hardware compatibility with the original Pico.

specifications.CPU: Dual-core ARM Cortex-M33 or RISC-V Hazard3, 150 MHz
specifications.Security Features: Arm TrustZone, boot signing, OTP, SHA-256, TRNG

codeSnippet.title: Dual-Core Example
codeSnippet.code:
# Pico 2 dual-core example with MicroPython
import _thread
from machine import Pin
import time

led = Pin(25, Pin.OUT)

def core1_task():
    """Task running on second core"""
    while True:
        print("Core 1 running")
        time.sleep(2)

# Start task on second core
_thread.start_new_thread(core1_task, ())

# Main core task
while True:
    led.toggle()
    print("Core 0 running")
    time.sleep(1)

components.name: Raspberry Pi Sense HAT
components.shortDesc: Multi-sensor HAT with 8x8 LED matrix, gyroscope, accelerometer, and environmental sensors
components.fullDescription: The Sense HAT is a versatile board featuring an 8x8 RGB LED matrix, a five-button joystick, and multiple sensors including gyroscope, accelerometer, magnetometer, temperature, barometric pressure, and humidity. Originally designed for the Astro Pi mission on the International Space Station.

gpioRequirements.Additional: All GPIO pins available via passthrough header

codeSnippet.title: Sense HAT Environmental Data
codeSnippet.code:
from sense_hat import SenseHat
import time

sense = SenseHat()
sense.clear()

while True:
    # Read sensors
    temp = sense.get_temperature()
    humidity = sense.get_humidity()
    pressure = sense.get_pressure()
    
    # Display on LED matrix
    sense.show_message(
        f"T:{temp:.1f}C H:{humidity:.1f}%",
        scroll_speed=0.05,
        text_colour=[255, 255, 0]
    )
    
    print(f"Temp: {temp:.1f}°C")
    print(f"Humidity: {humidity:.1f}%")
    print(f"Pressure: {pressure:.1f} mbar")
    
    time.sleep(5)

components.name: Raspberry Pi Camera Module 3
components.shortDesc: 12MP autofocus camera with HDR support for high-quality photos and videos
components.fullDescription: The Camera Module 3 features a 12-megapixel Sony IMX708 sensor with autofocus, HDR support, and improved low-light performance. Connects via the CSI camera port and supports both photos and 1080p video recording.

codeSnippet.title: Capture Photo with Camera
codeSnippet.code:
from picamera2 import Picamera2
import time

# Initialize camera
camera = Picamera2()

# Configure for photo capture
config = camera.create_still_configuration()
camera.configure(config)

# Start and capture
camera.start()
time.sleep(2)  # Allow camera to adjust

camera.capture_file("photo.jpg")
print("Photo captured!")

camera.stop()

components.name: M.2 HAT+ for Raspberry Pi 5
components.shortDesc: PCIe HAT+ for connecting NVMe SSDs and AI accelerators to Raspberry Pi 5
components.fullDescription: The M.2 HAT+ enables high-speed NVMe SSD storage and AI accelerator connections via the PCIe 2.0 interface on Raspberry Pi 5. Supports M.2 2230 and 2242 form factors with up to 500 MB/s transfer speeds.

specifications.Compatible Devices: NVMe SSDs, AI accelerators (e.g., Coral TPU)

codeSnippet.title: Enable PCIe and Check NVMe
codeSnippet.code:
# Enable PCIe on Raspberry Pi 5
sudo raspi-config nonint do_boot_wait 0

# Reboot to apply changes
sudo reboot

# After reboot, check NVMe detection
lsblk

# Expected output should show nvme0n1 device
# Format and mount NVMe drive
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/nvme0n1
sudo mount /dev/nvme0n1 /mnt/nvme

components.name: Raspberry Pi Active Cooler
components.shortDesc: Official aluminum heatsink with integrated fan for optimal Pi 5 cooling
components.fullDescription: The Active Cooler is designed specifically for Raspberry Pi 5, combining an aluminum heatsink with a temperature-controlled fan. Connects to the dedicated fan connector for automatic speed adjustment based on CPU temperature.

gpioRequirements.GPIO Clearance: Does not interfere with GPIO header

codeSnippet.title: Monitor Temperature
codeSnippet.code:
# Monitor CPU temperature with Active Cooler
watch -n 1 'vcgencmd measure_temp'

# Expected output with Active Cooler:
# temp=45.0'C (vs 70-80'C without cooling)

# Python script for temperature monitoring
python3 << EOF
import os
import time

while True:
    temp = os.popen('vcgencmd measure_temp').readline()
    print(f"CPU: {temp.strip()}")
    time.sleep(2)
EOF

components.name: PoE+ HAT
components.shortDesc: Power over Ethernet HAT delivering up to 25W for Pi 4 and earlier models
components.fullDescription: The PoE+ HAT allows your Raspberry Pi to receive power and network connectivity through a single Ethernet cable. Delivers up to 25W of power, suitable for Pi 4 with peripherals. Includes a cooling fan and supports 802.3at PoE+ standard.

gpioRequirements.Pins Used: GPIO header for power and fan control
gpioRequirements.GPIO Passthrough: All pins available via top header

codeSnippet.title: Check PoE Status
codeSnippet.code:
# Check if PoE HAT is detected
vcgencmd get_throttled

# Monitor power supply status
vcgencmd pmic_read_adc

# Check fan status
cat /sys/class/thermal/cooling_device0/cur_state

# Expected output: 0-3 (fan speed levels)

components.name: Official 7-inch Touchscreen Display
components.shortDesc: 800x480 capacitive touch display with 10-finger multi-touch support
components.fullDescription: The official Raspberry Pi 7-inch touchscreen provides an intuitive touch interface for your projects. Features a 800x480 resolution display with10-finger capacitive multi-touch, connects via DSI port, and is powered directly from the Pi's GPIO pins. Perfect for creating tablets, infotainment systems, and interactive displays.

codeSnippet.title: Touch Event Detection
codeSnippet.code:
# Simple touchscreen input demo
import pygame
import sys

# Initialize Pygame
pygame.init()
screen = pygame.display.set_mode((800, 480))
pygame.display.set_caption('Touch Demo')
pygame.mouse.set_visible(True)

# Colors
WHITE = (255, 255, 255)
BLUE = (0, 0, 255)

running = True
while running:
    for event in pygame.event.get():
        if event.type == pygame.QUIT:
            running = False
        
        if event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONDOWN:
            pos = pygame.mouse.get_pos()
            print(f"Touch at: {pos}")
            # Draw circle at touch point
            pygame.draw.circle(screen, BLUE, pos, 20)
            pygame.display.flip()
    
    screen.fill(WHITE)
    font = pygame.font.Font(None, 36)
    text = font.render('Touch the screen!', True, (0, 0, 0))
    screen.blit(text, (250, 220))
    pygame.display.flip()

pygame.quit()
sys.exit()

components.name: Official USB-C Power Supply (5V/3A)
components.shortDesc: Official 5V 3A USB-C power supply for Raspberry Pi 4 and Pi 400
components.fullDescription: The official Raspberry Pi USB-C power supply delivers stable 5.1V at 3A (15.3W) specifically designed for Raspberry Pi 4 Model B and Pi 400. Features over-current, over-voltage, and short-circuit protection with 1.5m cable length.

codeSnippet.title: Check Power Supply Status
codeSnippet.code:
# Check current voltage and throttling status
vcgencmd measure_volts
vcgencmd get_throttled

# Expected output with good power:
# volt=5.10V
# throttled=0x0 (no throttling)

# Monitor power supply quality
vcgencmd pmic_read_adc EXT5V_V

# Check if under-voltage occurred
dmesg | grep -i voltage
